meta.name: AEL Engineering Academy
meta.description: Learn LLM Engineering from zero to production
meta.author: Ayman Elmasry — AEL Digital Studio

modules.name: Foundations
modules.description: Understand what LLMs are and how they work

modules.name: Core Techniques
modules.description: Master the essential LLM engineering techniques

concepts.name: What is an LLM?
concepts.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) are neural networks trained on massive text corpora to understand and generate human language. Unlike traditional ML models that are trained for specific tasks, LLMs are general-purpose models that can be adapted to many tasks through prompting or fine-tuning.
concepts.referenceContent: Large Language Models (LLMs) are neural networks trained on massive text corpora to understand and generate human language. Unlike traditional ML models that are trained for specific tasks, LLMs are general-purpose models that can be adapted to many tasks through prompting or fine-tuning.
keyPoints: LLMs are trained on trillions of tokens
They can perform many tasks without task-specific training
They are the foundation of modern AI applications

concepts.name: LLM vs Traditional ML
concepts.description: Traditional ML models are trained for specific tasks with labeled data. LLMs are pre-trained on general text and can be adapted to many tasks. This is a fundamental shift in how we build AI systems.
concepts.referenceContent: Traditional ML models are trained for specific tasks with labeled data. LLMs are pre-trained on general text and can be adapted to many tasks. This is a fundamental shift in how we build AI systems.
keyPoints: Traditional ML: Task-specific, labeled data, small datasets
LLMs can be prompted without training
LLMs can be fine-tuned for specific tasks

concepts.name: Training Process
concepts.description: LLM training involves two main stages: pre-training and fine-tuning. Pre-training teaches the model language patterns. Fine-tuning adapts it to specific tasks or domains.
concepts.referenceContent: LLM training involves two main stages: pre-training and fine-tuning. Pre-training teaches the model language patterns. Fine-tuning adapts it to specific tasks or domains.
keyPoints: Pre-training: Learn language patterns from massive text
Fine-tuning: Adapt to specific tasks or domains
RLHF: Align model with human preferences
Deployment: Serve the model to users

concepts.name: Self-Attention Mechanism
concepts.description: Self-attention allows each token in a sequence to attend to every other token, computing relevance scores. It uses Query, Key, and Value matrices to determine how much focus each token should place on others.
concepts.referenceContent: Self-attention allows each token in a sequence to attend to every other token, computing relevance scores. It uses Query, Key, and Value matrices to determine how much focus each token should place on others.
keyPoints: Q (Query), K (Key), V (Value) matrices are learned
Attention scores are computed as softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))
Each token gets a weighted sum of all other tokens' values

concepts.name: Multi-Head Attention
concepts.description: Multi-head attention runs multiple attention operations in parallel, each learning different relationship patterns. The outputs are concatenated and linearly transformed to produce the final attention output.
concepts.referenceContent: Multi-head attention runs multiple attention operations in parallel, each learning different relationship patterns. The outputs are concatenated and linearly transformed to produce the final attention output.
keyPoints: Multiple heads learn different types of relationships
Improves model's ability to focus on diverse information

concepts.name: Positional Encoding
concepts.description: Since transformers process all tokens simultaneously, they need explicit position information. Positional encoding adds position signals to token embeddings so the model knows token order.
concepts.referenceContent: Since transformers process all tokens simultaneously, they need explicit position information. Positional encoding adds position signals to token embeddings so the model knows token order.
keyPoints: Transformers have no inherent notion of sequence order
Sinusoidal functions or learned embeddings encode position
Relative positional encoding (RoPE) is now preferred
Enables the model to understand word order

concepts.name: Encoder vs Decoder vs Encoder-Decoder
concepts.description: Encoder models (BERT) process input bidirectionally. Decoder models (GPT) generate text autoregressively. Encoder-decoder models (T5, BART) use both for tasks like translation.
concepts.referenceContent: Encoder models (BERT) process input bidirectionally. Decoder models (GPT) generate text autoregressively. Encoder-decoder models (T5, BART) use both for tasks like translation.
keyPoints: Encoder: Bidirectional, good for understanding tasks
Decoder: Autoregressive, good for generation tasks
Most modern LLMs use decoder-only architecture

concepts.name: What is Tokenization?
concepts.description: Tokenization is the process of splitting text into tokens that the model can process. It's a critical preprocessing step that directly affects model performance, vocabulary size, and the ability to handle different languages.
concepts.referenceContent: Tokenization is the process of splitting text into tokens that the model can process. It's a critical preprocessing step that directly affects model performance, vocabulary size, and the ability to handle different languages.
keyPoints: Converts raw text into numerical token IDs
Determines the vocabulary the model uses
Affects context window utilization and cost
Different tokenizers suit different use cases

concepts.name: BPE (Byte Pair Encoding)
concepts.description: BPE is the most common tokenization algorithm used in modern LLMs. It starts with individual characters and iteratively merges the most frequent pairs, building up a vocabulary of subword tokens.
concepts.referenceContent: BPE is the most common tokenization algorithm used in modern LLMs. It starts with individual characters and iteratively merges the most frequent pairs, building up a vocabulary of subword tokens.
keyPoints: Iteratively merges most frequent byte pairs
Used by GPT, LLaMA, and most modern LLMs

concepts.name: WordPiece and SentencePiece
concepts.description: WordPiece is similar to BPE but uses a likelihood-based merging criterion. SentencePiece is a language-agnostic tokenizer that works directly on raw text without preprocessing.
concepts.referenceContent: WordPiece is similar to BPE but uses a likelihood-based merging criterion. SentencePiece is a language-agnostic tokenizer that works directly on raw text without preprocessing.
keyPoints: WordPiece: Used by BERT, uses log-likelihood merging
SentencePiece supports byte fallback for unknown tokens

concepts.name: Tokenization Impact on Models
concepts.description: Tokenization directly impacts context window utilization, API costs, model accuracy, and multilingual performance. Poor tokenization can waste context window space and increase costs.
concepts.referenceContent: Tokenization directly impacts context window utilization, API costs, model accuracy, and multilingual performance. Poor tokenization can waste context window space and increase costs.
keyPoints: Common words get fewer tokens, rare words get more
Code and technical content may be tokenized poorly

concepts.name: Prompt Engineering Fundamentals
concepts.description: Prompt engineering is the art and science of designing inputs that guide LLMs to produce desired outputs. Good prompts are clear, specific, and provide enough context for the model to understand the task.
concepts.referenceContent: Prompt engineering is the art and science of designing inputs that guide LLMs to produce desired outputs. Good prompts are clear, specific, and provide enough context for the model to understand the task.
keyPoints: Context and examples guide the model

concepts.name: Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Prompting
concepts.description: Zero-shot prompting asks the model to perform a task without examples. Few-shot prompting provides examples of the desired input-output format. Few-shot typically produces more consistent and accurate results.
concepts.referenceContent: Zero-shot prompting asks the model to perform a task without examples. Few-shot prompting provides examples of the desired input-output format. Few-shot typically produces more consistent and accurate results.
keyPoints: Zero-shot: No examples, relies on model's pre-training
Example quality matters more than quantity
Demonstrate the exact format you want

concepts.name: Chain-of-Thought Prompting
concepts.description: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting asks the model to show its reasoning step by step. This dramatically improves performance on complex reasoning tasks like math, logic, and multi-step problems.
concepts.referenceContent: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting asks the model to show its reasoning step by step. This dramatically improves performance on complex reasoning tasks like math, logic, and multi-step problems.
keyPoints: Ask model to 'think step by step'
Can be combined with few-shot examples

concepts.name: Advanced Prompt Techniques
concepts.description: Techniques like self-consistency, tree-of-thought, and prompt chaining enable more complex reasoning. Structured output formats (JSON, XML) make prompts more parseable and reliable.
concepts.referenceContent: Techniques like self-consistency, tree-of-thought, and prompt chaining enable more complex reasoning. Structured output formats (JSON, XML) make prompts more parseable and reliable.
keyPoints: Prompt chaining: Break complex tasks into steps
Self-consistency: Sample multiple paths, take majority vote
Role-playing: Assign expertise to the model

concepts.name: When to Fine-tune
concepts.description: Fine-tuning is necessary when prompting alone doesn't achieve the desired performance. It's useful for domain-specific tasks, consistent output formatting, reducing latency, and reducing token costs.
concepts.referenceContent: Fine-tuning is necessary when prompting alone doesn't achieve the desired performance. It's useful for domain-specific tasks, consistent output formatting, reducing latency, and reducing token costs.
keyPoints: Prompting fails on specialized domain tasks
When consistent output format is critical
When latency needs to be reduced
When you need to reduce per-request token costs

concepts.name: Dataset Preparation
concepts.description: Fine-tuning requires high-quality training data in the right format. Data should be representative of the target task, properly formatted, and split into train/validation sets.
concepts.referenceContent: Fine-tuning requires high-quality training data in the right format. Data should be representative of the target task, properly formatted, and split into train/validation sets.
keyPoints: Data quality matters more than quantity

concepts.name: Fine-tuning Methods
concepts.description: Full fine-tuning updates all model parameters. LoRA and QLoRA update only a small subset, making fine-tuning accessible on consumer hardware. PEFT methods reduce computational requirements dramatically.
concepts.referenceContent: Full fine-tuning updates all model parameters. LoRA and QLoRA update only a small subset, making fine-tuning accessible on consumer hardware. PEFT methods reduce computational requirements dramatically.

concepts.name: Evaluation and Optimization
concepts.description: After fine-tuning, evaluate the model on held-out data. Monitor metrics like loss, accuracy, and task-specific metrics. Adjust hyperparameters like learning rate, batch size, and epochs.
concepts.referenceContent: After fine-tuning, evaluate the model on held-out data. Monitor metrics like loss, accuracy, and task-specific metrics. Adjust hyperparameters like learning rate, batch size, and epochs.
keyPoints: Track training and validation loss curves

concepts.name: RAG Fundamentals
concepts.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines LLMs with external knowledge retrieval. Instead of relying solely on the model's training data, RAG retrieves relevant documents at query time and includes them in the prompt.
concepts.referenceContent: Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines LLMs with external knowledge retrieval. Instead of relying solely on the model's training data, RAG retrieves relevant documents at query time and includes them in the prompt.
keyPoints: Reduces hallucination by grounding in real data
Keeps LLM responses current and accurate
Combines the best of retrieval and generation

concepts.name: Document Chunking
concepts.description: Documents must be split into chunks for embedding and retrieval. Chunk size, overlap, and splitting strategy significantly impact retrieval quality.
concepts.referenceContent: Documents must be split into chunks for embedding and retrieval. Chunk size, overlap, and splitting strategy significantly impact retrieval quality.
keyPoints: Overlap prevents information loss at boundaries

concepts.name: Vector Embeddings and Search
concepts.description: Text is converted to numerical vectors (embeddings) using embedding models. Similarity search finds the most relevant chunks using cosine similarity or dot product.
concepts.referenceContent: Text is converted to numerical vectors (embeddings) using embedding models. Similarity search finds the most relevant chunks using cosine similarity or dot product.
keyPoints: Embedding models convert text to dense vectors
Vector databases enable fast similarity search
Hybrid search combines keyword and semantic search

concepts.name: Advanced RAG Techniques
concepts.description: Advanced RAG improves retrieval quality through query expansion, re-ranking, hybrid search, and iterative retrieval. These techniques significantly improve answer accuracy.
concepts.referenceContent: Advanced RAG improves retrieval quality through query expansion, re-ranking, hybrid search, and iterative retrieval. These techniques significantly improve answer accuracy.
keyPoints: Query expansion: Reformulate queries for better retrieval
Re-ranking: Score retrieved chunks by relevance
Hybrid search: Combine BM25 with vector search
Iterative RAG: Retrieve, generate, retrieve again

concepts.name: LLM Agents
concepts.description: LLM agents are autonomous systems that use LLMs as a reasoning engine to plan, execute, and iterate on tasks. They combine LLM reasoning with tool use to accomplish complex goals.
concepts.referenceContent: LLM agents are autonomous systems that use LLMs as a reasoning engine to plan, execute, and iterate on tasks. They combine LLM reasoning with tool use to accomplish complex goals.
keyPoints: Agents use LLMs for planning and decision-making
They can break complex tasks into subtasks
They use tools to interact with external systems
They can observe results and adjust their approach

concepts.name: Tool Calling
concepts.description: Tool calling allows LLMs to invoke external functions and APIs. The model decides which tools to use and provides parameters, then processes the tool's output.
concepts.referenceContent: Tool calling allows LLMs to invoke external functions and APIs. The model decides which tools to use and provides parameters, then processes the tool's output.
keyPoints: Define tools with names, descriptions, and parameters
LLM selects appropriate tools based on the task
Tool outputs are fed back to the LLM for reasoning

concepts.name: Agent Architectures
concepts.description: Different agent architectures suit different tasks: ReAct (reasoning + acting), Plan-and-Execute, Multi-Agent systems, and hierarchical agents.
concepts.referenceContent: Different agent architectures suit different tasks: ReAct (reasoning + acting), Plan-and-Execute, Multi-Agent systems, and hierarchical agents.
keyPoints: ReAct: Interleaves reasoning and action steps
Hierarchical: Manager agent delegates to workers

concepts.name: Error Handling in Agents
concepts.description: Agents must handle tool failures, unexpected outputs, loops, and resource limits gracefully. Robust error handling is critical for reliable agent behavior.
concepts.referenceContent: Agents must handle tool failures, unexpected outputs, loops, and resource limits gracefully. Robust error handling is critical for reliable agent behavior.

concepts.name: Evaluation Fundamentals
concepts.description: Evaluation measures how well your LLM application performs. It covers accuracy, relevance, safety, consistency, and user satisfaction. A good evaluation framework catches regressions and guides improvements.
concepts.referenceContent: Evaluation measures how well your LLM application performs. It covers accuracy, relevance, safety, consistency, and user satisfaction. A good evaluation framework catches regressions and guides improvements.

concepts.name: Automated Metrics
concepts.description: Automated metrics include exact match, BLEU, ROUGE, faithfulness scores, and task-specific metrics. They enable fast, repeatable evaluation at scale.
concepts.referenceContent: Automated metrics include exact match, BLEU, ROUGE, faithfulness scores, and task-specific metrics. They enable fast, repeatable evaluation at scale.

concepts.name: LLM-as-a-Judge
concepts.description: Using an LLM to evaluate outputs of another LLM. Cost-effective and scalable, but requires careful prompt design and calibration against human judgments.
concepts.referenceContent: Using an LLM to evaluate outputs of another LLM. Cost-effective and scalable, but requires careful prompt design and calibration against human judgments.
keyPoints: Watch for LLM biases in evaluation

concepts.name: Human Evaluation
concepts.description: Human evaluation provides ground truth for quality. It's essential for subjective tasks and for calibrating automated metrics. Design clear evaluation guidelines and inter-annotator agreement.
concepts.referenceContent: Human evaluation provides ground truth for quality. It's essential for subjective tasks and for calibrating automated metrics. Design clear evaluation guidelines and inter-annotator agreement.
keyPoints: Use human eval to validate automated metrics

concepts.name: LLM Application Architecture
concepts.description: Production LLM applications need proper architecture including API design, middleware, caching, rate limiting, and fallback strategies. Design for scalability and reliability from the start.
concepts.referenceContent: Production LLM applications need proper architecture including API design, middleware, caching, rate limiting, and fallback strategies. Design for scalability and reliability from the start.
keyPoints: Separate concerns: API, business logic, LLM calls
Implement proper error handling and retries
Use async processing for long-running tasks

concepts.name: Caching Strategies
concepts.description: Caching LLM responses dramatically reduces costs and latency. Implement semantic caching for similar queries and exact caching for identical requests.
concepts.referenceContent: Caching LLM responses dramatically reduces costs and latency. Implement semantic caching for similar queries and exact caching for identical requests.
keyPoints: Exact match caching for identical prompts

concepts.name: Cost and Latency Optimization
concepts.description: Optimize costs through model selection, prompt optimization, response streaming, batching, and token efficiency. Balance quality requirements with budget constraints.
concepts.referenceContent: Optimize costs through model selection, prompt optimization, response streaming, batching, and token efficiency. Balance quality requirements with budget constraints.
keyPoints: Choose appropriate model for each task
Monitor and alert on cost anomalies

concepts.name: Monitoring and Observability
concepts.description: Monitor LLM applications for quality, performance, costs, and safety. Implement logging, tracing, alerting, and dashboards.
concepts.referenceContent: Monitor LLM applications for quality, performance, costs, and safety. Implement logging, tracing, alerting, and dashboards.
keyPoints: Track latency, cost, and quality metrics

concepts.name: LLM Safety Risks
concepts.description: LLMs can produce harmful, biased, false, or manipulative content. Understanding these risks is essential for building responsible AI systems that protect users.
concepts.referenceContent: LLMs can produce harmful, biased, false, or manipulative content. Understanding these risks is essential for building responsible AI systems that protect users.
keyPoints: Bias: Reflecting biases in training data
Data leakage: Revealing sensitive training data

concepts.name: Content Filtering
concepts.description: Content filters prevent harmful inputs from reaching the model and harmful outputs from reaching users. Implement multi-layer filtering for both input and output.
concepts.referenceContent: Content filters prevent harmful inputs from reaching the model and harmful outputs from reaching users. Implement multi-layer filtering for both input and output.
keyPoints: Use both keyword and classifier-based filtering
Update filters as new risks emerge

concepts.name: Guardrails
concepts.description: Guardrails are constraints and checks that ensure LLM outputs meet safety, quality, and compliance requirements. They act as a safety net around the model.
concepts.referenceContent: Guardrails are constraints and checks that ensure LLM outputs meet safety, quality, and compliance requirements. They act as a safety net around the model.

concepts.name: Adversarial Testing
concepts.description: Red-teaming tests your system against adversarial attacks including prompt injection, jailbreaking, and data extraction. Regular testing is essential for safety.
concepts.referenceContent: Red-teaming tests your system against adversarial attacks including prompt injection, jailbreaking, and data extraction. Regular testing is essential for safety.

concepts.name: Model Distillation
concepts.description: Model distillation trains smaller models to mimic larger ones. Knowledge transfer from teacher to student models enables deployment on resource-constrained environments while preserving performance.
concepts.referenceContent: Model distillation trains smaller models to mimic larger ones. Knowledge transfer from teacher to student models enables deployment on resource-constrained environments while preserving performance.

concepts.name: Function Calling Patterns
concepts.description: Advanced function calling enables LLMs to interact with complex APIs and systems. Design patterns include chained calls, parallel execution, and conditional tool use.
concepts.referenceContent: Advanced function calling enables LLMs to interact with complex APIs and systems. Design patterns include chained calls, parallel execution, and conditional tool use.
keyPoints: Conditional tool selection based on context

concepts.name: Multi-Modal LLMs
concepts.description: Multi-modal LLMs process text, images, audio, and video. They enable richer interactions and more capable AI applications across different media types.
concepts.referenceContent: Multi-modal LLMs process text, images, audio, and video. They enable richer interactions and more capable AI applications across different media types.

concepts.name: Local and Edge Deployment
concepts.description: Deploying LLMs locally or on edge devices reduces latency, improves privacy, and eliminates API costs. Quantization and optimization make this feasible on consumer hardware.
concepts.referenceContent: Deploying LLMs locally or on edge devices reduces latency, improves privacy, and eliminates API costs. Quantization and optimization make this feasible on consumer hardware.

concepts.name: Project Planning
concepts.description: Effective project planning defines scope, architecture, and milestones. Start with clear requirements, design the system architecture, and plan iterations.
concepts.referenceContent: Effective project planning defines scope, architecture, and milestones. Start with clear requirements, design the system architecture, and plan iterations.
keyPoints: Define clear problem statement and success criteria

concepts.name: System Architecture Design
concepts.description: Design a complete system with proper separation of concerns. Include data pipeline, LLM integration, evaluation, safety, and monitoring layers.
concepts.referenceContent: Design a complete system with proper separation of concerns. Include data pipeline, LLM integration, evaluation, safety, and monitoring layers.

concepts.name: Implementation Best Practices
concepts.description: Follow engineering best practices: version control, testing, code review, documentation, and CI/CD. Build incrementally with working versions at each stage.
concepts.referenceContent: Follow engineering best practices: version control, testing, code review, documentation, and CI/CD. Build incrementally with working versions at each stage.
keyPoints: Start with a minimum viable product

concepts.name: Presentation and Communication
concepts.description: Communicate technical decisions clearly. Explain trade-offs, demonstrate working systems, and handle questions about architecture choices.
concepts.referenceContent: Communicate technical decisions clearly. Explain trade-offs, demonstrate working systems, and handle questions about architecture choices.
keyPoints: Tell a clear story about the project
Discuss what you would do differently

learningOutcomes.statement: Explain what an LLM is in simple terms
Differentiate between LLM and traditional ML models
Set up a Python environment for LLM development
Make a basic API call to an LLM
Understand multi-head attention and its purpose
Explain positional encoding and why it's needed
Explain what tokenization is and why it matters
Compare different tokenization algorithms (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece)
Understand how tokenization affects model performance
Master zero-shot and few-shot prompting techniques
Design effective system prompts for different tasks
Implement vector embeddings and similarity search
Test for adversarial attacks and vulnerabilities
Explore local and on-device LLM deployment
Design a complete LLM application from scratch
Implement all components: RAG, agents, evaluation
Apply all course knowledge to a real problem

referenceTopics.name: What is an LLM?
referenceTopics.content: Large Language Models (LLMs) are neural networks trained on massive text corpora to understand and generate human language. Unlike traditional ML models that are trained for specific tasks, LLMs are general-purpose models that can be adapted to many tasks through prompting or fine-tuning.
keyPoints: LLMs are trained on trillions of tokens
They can perform many tasks without task-specific training
They are the foundation of modern AI applications

referenceTopics.name: LLM vs Traditional ML
referenceTopics.content: Traditional ML models are trained for specific tasks with labeled data. LLMs are pre-trained on general text and can be adapted to many tasks. This is a fundamental shift in how we build AI systems.
keyPoints: Traditional ML: Task-specific, labeled data, small datasets
LLMs can be prompted without training
LLMs can be fine-tuned for specific tasks

referenceTopics.name: Training Process
referenceTopics.content: LLM training involves two main stages: pre-training and fine-tuning. Pre-training teaches the model language patterns. Fine-tuning adapts it to specific tasks or domains.
keyPoints: Pre-training: Learn language patterns from massive text
Fine-tuning: Adapt to specific tasks or domains
RLHF: Align model with human preferences
Deployment: Serve the model to users

referenceTopics.name: Self-Attention Mechanism
referenceTopics.content: Self-attention allows each token in a sequence to attend to every other token, computing relevance scores. It uses Query, Key, and Value matrices to determine how much focus each token should place on others.
keyPoints: Q (Query), K (Key), V (Value) matrices are learned
Attention scores are computed as softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))
Each token gets a weighted sum of all other tokens' values

referenceTopics.name: Multi-Head Attention
referenceTopics.content: Multi-head attention runs multiple attention operations in parallel, each learning different relationship patterns. The outputs are concatenated and linearly transformed to produce the final attention output.
keyPoints: Multiple heads learn different types of relationships
Improves model's ability to focus on diverse information

referenceTopics.name: Positional Encoding
referenceTopics.content: Since transformers process all tokens simultaneously, they need explicit position information. Positional encoding adds position signals to token embeddings so the model knows token order.
keyPoints: Transformers have no inherent notion of sequence order
Sinusoidal functions or learned embeddings encode position
Relative positional encoding (RoPE) is now preferred
Enables the model to understand word order

referenceTopics.name: Encoder vs Decoder vs Encoder-Decoder
referenceTopics.content: Encoder models (BERT) process input bidirectionally. Decoder models (GPT) generate text autoregressively. Encoder-decoder models (T5, BART) use both for tasks like translation.
keyPoints: Encoder: Bidirectional, good for understanding tasks
Decoder: Autoregressive, good for generation tasks
Most modern LLMs use decoder-only architecture

referenceTopics.name: What is Tokenization?
referenceTopics.content: Tokenization is the process of splitting text into tokens that the model can process. It's a critical preprocessing step that directly affects model performance, vocabulary size, and the ability to handle different languages.
keyPoints: Converts raw text into numerical token IDs
Determines the vocabulary the model uses
Affects context window utilization and cost
Different tokenizers suit different use cases

referenceTopics.name: BPE (Byte Pair Encoding)
referenceTopics.content: BPE is the most common tokenization algorithm used in modern LLMs. It starts with individual characters and iteratively merges the most frequent pairs, building up a vocabulary of subword tokens.
keyPoints: Iteratively merges most frequent byte pairs
Used by GPT, LLaMA, and most modern LLMs

referenceTopics.name: WordPiece and SentencePiece
referenceTopics.content: WordPiece is similar to BPE but uses a likelihood-based merging criterion. SentencePiece is a language-agnostic tokenizer that works directly on raw text without preprocessing.
keyPoints: WordPiece: Used by BERT, uses log-likelihood merging
SentencePiece supports byte fallback for unknown tokens

referenceTopics.name: Tokenization Impact on Models
referenceTopics.content: Tokenization directly impacts context window utilization, API costs, model accuracy, and multilingual performance. Poor tokenization can waste context window space and increase costs.
keyPoints: Common words get fewer tokens, rare words get more
Code and technical content may be tokenized poorly

referenceTopics.name: Prompt Engineering Fundamentals
referenceTopics.content: Prompt engineering is the art and science of designing inputs that guide LLMs to produce desired outputs. Good prompts are clear, specific, and provide enough context for the model to understand the task.
keyPoints: Context and examples guide the model

referenceTopics.name: Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Prompting
referenceTopics.content: Zero-shot prompting asks the model to perform a task without examples. Few-shot prompting provides examples of the desired input-output format. Few-shot typically produces more consistent and accurate results.
keyPoints: Zero-shot: No examples, relies on model's pre-training
Example quality matters more than quantity
Demonstrate the exact format you want

referenceTopics.name: Chain-of-Thought Prompting
referenceTopics.content: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting asks the model to show its reasoning step by step. This dramatically improves performance on complex reasoning tasks like math, logic, and multi-step problems.
keyPoints: Ask model to 'think step by step'
Can be combined with few-shot examples

referenceTopics.name: Advanced Prompt Techniques
referenceTopics.content: Techniques like self-consistency, tree-of-thought, and prompt chaining enable more complex reasoning. Structured output formats (JSON, XML) make prompts more parseable and reliable.
keyPoints: Prompt chaining: Break complex tasks into steps
Self-consistency: Sample multiple paths, take majority vote
Role-playing: Assign expertise to the model

referenceTopics.name: When to Fine-tune
referenceTopics.content: Fine-tuning is necessary when prompting alone doesn't achieve the desired performance. It's useful for domain-specific tasks, consistent output formatting, reducing latency, and reducing token costs.
keyPoints: Prompting fails on specialized domain tasks
When consistent output format is critical
When latency needs to be reduced
When you need to reduce per-request token costs

referenceTopics.name: Dataset Preparation
referenceTopics.content: Fine-tuning requires high-quality training data in the right format. Data should be representative of the target task, properly formatted, and split into train/validation sets.
keyPoints: Data quality matters more than quantity

referenceTopics.name: Fine-tuning Methods
referenceTopics.content: Full fine-tuning updates all model parameters. LoRA and QLoRA update only a small subset, making fine-tuning accessible on consumer hardware. PEFT methods reduce computational requirements dramatically.

referenceTopics.name: Evaluation and Optimization
referenceTopics.content: After fine-tuning, evaluate the model on held-out data. Monitor metrics like loss, accuracy, and task-specific metrics. Adjust hyperparameters like learning rate, batch size, and epochs.
keyPoints: Track training and validation loss curves

referenceTopics.name: RAG Fundamentals
referenceTopics.content: Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines LLMs with external knowledge retrieval. Instead of relying solely on the model's training data, RAG retrieves relevant documents at query time and includes them in the prompt.
keyPoints: Reduces hallucination by grounding in real data
Keeps LLM responses current and accurate
Combines the best of retrieval and generation

referenceTopics.name: Document Chunking
referenceTopics.content: Documents must be split into chunks for embedding and retrieval. Chunk size, overlap, and splitting strategy significantly impact retrieval quality.
keyPoints: Overlap prevents information loss at boundaries

referenceTopics.name: Vector Embeddings and Search
referenceTopics.content: Text is converted to numerical vectors (embeddings) using embedding models. Similarity search finds the most relevant chunks using cosine similarity or dot product.
keyPoints: Embedding models convert text to dense vectors
Vector databases enable fast similarity search
Hybrid search combines keyword and semantic search

referenceTopics.name: Advanced RAG Techniques
referenceTopics.content: Advanced RAG improves retrieval quality through query expansion, re-ranking, hybrid search, and iterative retrieval. These techniques significantly improve answer accuracy.
keyPoints: Query expansion: Reformulate queries for better retrieval
Re-ranking: Score retrieved chunks by relevance
Hybrid search: Combine BM25 with vector search
Iterative RAG: Retrieve, generate, retrieve again

referenceTopics.name: LLM Agents
referenceTopics.content: LLM agents are autonomous systems that use LLMs as a reasoning engine to plan, execute, and iterate on tasks. They combine LLM reasoning with tool use to accomplish complex goals.
keyPoints: Agents use LLMs for planning and decision-making
They can break complex tasks into subtasks
They use tools to interact with external systems
They can observe results and adjust their approach

referenceTopics.name: Tool Calling
referenceTopics.content: Tool calling allows LLMs to invoke external functions and APIs. The model decides which tools to use and provides parameters, then processes the tool's output.
keyPoints: Define tools with names, descriptions, and parameters
LLM selects appropriate tools based on the task
Tool outputs are fed back to the LLM for reasoning

referenceTopics.name: Agent Architectures
referenceTopics.content: Different agent architectures suit different tasks: ReAct (reasoning + acting), Plan-and-Execute, Multi-Agent systems, and hierarchical agents.
keyPoints: ReAct: Interleaves reasoning and action steps
Hierarchical: Manager agent delegates to workers

referenceTopics.name: Error Handling in Agents
referenceTopics.content: Agents must handle tool failures, unexpected outputs, loops, and resource limits gracefully. Robust error handling is critical for reliable agent behavior.

referenceTopics.name: Evaluation Fundamentals
referenceTopics.content: Evaluation measures how well your LLM application performs. It covers accuracy, relevance, safety, consistency, and user satisfaction. A good evaluation framework catches regressions and guides improvements.

referenceTopics.name: Automated Metrics
referenceTopics.content: Automated metrics include exact match, BLEU, ROUGE, faithfulness scores, and task-specific metrics. They enable fast, repeatable evaluation at scale.

referenceTopics.name: LLM-as-a-Judge
referenceTopics.content: Using an LLM to evaluate outputs of another LLM. Cost-effective and scalable, but requires careful prompt design and calibration against human judgments.
keyPoints: Watch for LLM biases in evaluation

referenceTopics.name: Human Evaluation
referenceTopics.content: Human evaluation provides ground truth for quality. It's essential for subjective tasks and for calibrating automated metrics. Design clear evaluation guidelines and inter-annotator agreement.
keyPoints: Use human eval to validate automated metrics

referenceTopics.name: LLM Application Architecture
referenceTopics.content: Production LLM applications need proper architecture including API design, middleware, caching, rate limiting, and fallback strategies. Design for scalability and reliability from the start.
keyPoints: Separate concerns: API, business logic, LLM calls
Implement proper error handling and retries
Use async processing for long-running tasks

referenceTopics.name: Caching Strategies
referenceTopics.content: Caching LLM responses dramatically reduces costs and latency. Implement semantic caching for similar queries and exact caching for identical requests.
keyPoints: Exact match caching for identical prompts

referenceTopics.name: Cost and Latency Optimization
referenceTopics.content: Optimize costs through model selection, prompt optimization, response streaming, batching, and token efficiency. Balance quality requirements with budget constraints.
keyPoints: Choose appropriate model for each task
Monitor and alert on cost anomalies

referenceTopics.name: Monitoring and Observability
referenceTopics.content: Monitor LLM applications for quality, performance, costs, and safety. Implement logging, tracing, alerting, and dashboards.
keyPoints: Track latency, cost, and quality metrics

referenceTopics.name: LLM Safety Risks
referenceTopics.content: LLMs can produce harmful, biased, false, or manipulative content. Understanding these risks is essential for building responsible AI systems that protect users.
keyPoints: Bias: Reflecting biases in training data
Data leakage: Revealing sensitive training data

referenceTopics.name: Content Filtering
referenceTopics.content: Content filters prevent harmful inputs from reaching the model and harmful outputs from reaching users. Implement multi-layer filtering for both input and output.
keyPoints: Use both keyword and classifier-based filtering
Update filters as new risks emerge

referenceTopics.name: Guardrails
referenceTopics.content: Guardrails are constraints and checks that ensure LLM outputs meet safety, quality, and compliance requirements. They act as a safety net around the model.

referenceTopics.name: Adversarial Testing
referenceTopics.content: Red-teaming tests your system against adversarial attacks including prompt injection, jailbreaking, and data extraction. Regular testing is essential for safety.

referenceTopics.name: Model Distillation
referenceTopics.content: Model distillation trains smaller models to mimic larger ones. Knowledge transfer from teacher to student models enables deployment on resource-constrained environments while preserving performance.

referenceTopics.name: Function Calling Patterns
referenceTopics.content: Advanced function calling enables LLMs to interact with complex APIs and systems. Design patterns include chained calls, parallel execution, and conditional tool use.
keyPoints: Conditional tool selection based on context

referenceTopics.name: Multi-Modal LLMs
referenceTopics.content: Multi-modal LLMs process text, images, audio, and video. They enable richer interactions and more capable AI applications across different media types.

referenceTopics.name: Local and Edge Deployment
referenceTopics.content: Deploying LLMs locally or on edge devices reduces latency, improves privacy, and eliminates API costs. Quantization and optimization make this feasible on consumer hardware.

referenceTopics.name: Project Planning
referenceTopics.content: Effective project planning defines scope, architecture, and milestones. Start with clear requirements, design the system architecture, and plan iterations.
keyPoints: Define clear problem statement and success criteria

referenceTopics.name: System Architecture Design
referenceTopics.content: Design a complete system with proper separation of concerns. Include data pipeline, LLM integration, evaluation, safety, and monitoring layers.

referenceTopics.name: Implementation Best Practices
referenceTopics.content: Follow engineering best practices: version control, testing, code review, documentation, and CI/CD. Build incrementally with working versions at each stage.
keyPoints: Start with a minimum viable product

referenceTopics.name: Presentation and Communication
referenceTopics.content: Communicate technical decisions clearly. Explain trade-offs, demonstrate working systems, and handle questions about architecture choices.
keyPoints: Tell a clear story about the project
Discuss what you would do differently

exercises.title: Explain LLM to a Non-Technical Person
exercises.description: Write a 200-word explanation of what an LLM is for someone who has no technical background. Use analogies and simple language.

exercises.title: Compare LLM Providers
exercises.description: Create a comparison table of at least 3 LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google). Include: model names, context window, pricing, and strengths.

exercises.title: Set Up LLM Development Environment
exercises.description: Set up a Python environment with: Python 3.10+, pip, virtual environment, API keys for at least one provider, and a simple script that makes an API call.

exercises.title: Draw the Transformer Architecture
exercises.description: Create a detailed diagram of the transformer architecture showing encoder, decoder, self-attention, cross-attention, feed-forward layers, and residual connections.

exercises.title: Implement Self-Attention from Scratch
exercises.description: Write Python code implementing single-head self-attention using only NumPy. Include Q/K/V computation, attention scoring, and weighted output.

exercises.title: Compare Architectures
exercises.description: Research and compare at least 4 different transformer variants (BERT, GPT, T5, LLaMA). Document their architectural differences, use cases, and training objectives in a structured report.

exercises.title: Tokenize and Count
exercises.description: Write a script that tokenizes text using tiktoken (OpenAI's tokenizer) and shows the token count, token list, and token IDs for a given input text.

exercises.title: Compare Tokenizers
exercises.description: Compare how different tokenizers (tiktoken, HuggingFace, SentencePiece) tokenize the same text. Show token counts, token boundaries, and vocabulary sizes.

exercises.title: Implement Basic BPE
exercises.description: Implement a basic BPE tokenizer from scratch in Python. Train it on a small text corpus and demonstrate encoding/decoding of new text.

exercises.title: Write a System Prompt
exercises.description: Write a system prompt for a customer support chatbot that handles billing inquiries. Include role, tone, constraints, and output format.

exercises.title: Build a Few-Shot Classifier
exercises.description: Create a prompt that classifies customer reviews into positive, negative, and neutral using few-shot examples. Test with 10 different reviews.

exercises.title: Design a Prompt Chain
exercises.description: Design and implement a prompt chain that takes a research topic, generates questions, searches for answers, and produces a summary report. Each step should be a separate prompt.

exercises.title: Prepare a Fine-tuning Dataset
exercises.description: Create a dataset of 50 instruction-response pairs for a specific task (e.g., email classification, code review). Format it for OpenAI fine-tuning API.

exercises.title: Fine-tune with LoRA
exercises.description: Use Hugging Face PEFT to fine-tune a small language model (e.g., Phi-2) on a custom dataset using LoRA. Compare performance before and after fine-tuning.

exercises.title: Evaluate Fine-tuned Model
exercises.description: Fine-tune a model and build an evaluation pipeline that measures accuracy, consistency, and hallucination rate on a held-out test set. Generate a comparison report.

exercises.title: Build a Basic RAG Pipeline
exercises.description: Build a simple RAG system that ingests a PDF, chunks it, creates embeddings, and answers questions. Use ChromaDB for vector storage.

exercises.title: Compare Chunking Strategies
exercises.description: Implement 3 different chunking strategies (fixed-size, semantic, recursive) and compare their retrieval quality on a test dataset. Measure precision and recall.

exercises.title: Implement Hybrid Search RAG
exercises.description: Build a RAG system that combines BM25 keyword search with vector similarity search. Add re-ranking and compare results with pure vector search.

exercises.title: Build a Simple Tool-Calling Agent
exercises.description: Create an agent that can use a calculator tool and a web search tool. Implement the ReAct pattern with reasoning and action steps.

exercises.title: Build a Multi-Tool Agent
exercises.description: Extend the agent to use at least 5 tools: calculator, web search, file read/write, code execution, and API calls. Handle tool failures gracefully.

exercises.title: Build a Multi-Agent System
exercises.description: Create a system with 3 specialized agents (researcher, writer, reviewer) that collaborate to produce a report. Implement agent communication and task delegation.

exercises.title: Build an Evaluation Dataset
exercises.description: Create an evaluation dataset of 100 input-output pairs for a specific task. Include edge cases, ambiguous inputs, and diverse scenarios.

exercises.title: Implement LLM-as-a-Judge
exercises.description: Build an automated evaluation system using an LLM to judge output quality. Include scoring rubrics, calibration against human judgments, and bias detection.

exercises.title: Build an Evaluation Dashboard
exercises.description: Create a dashboard that tracks evaluation metrics over time, shows quality trends, highlights regressions, and generates evaluation reports.

exercises.title: Build a Simple LLM API
exercises.description: Create a FastAPI service that wraps an LLM API call with proper error handling, rate limiting, and logging. Include health checks and documentation.

exercises.title: Implement Caching Layer
exercises.description: Add semantic caching to the LLM API using Redis and embeddings. Measure cache hit rates and cost savings on a test dataset.

exercises.title: Build a Monitoring Dashboard
exercises.description: Create a dashboard that shows real-time metrics: request count, latency, costs, error rates, cache hit rates, and quality scores. Include alerting for anomalies.

exercises.title: Build a Content Filter
exercises.description: Implement a content filtering system that detects and blocks harmful inputs and outputs. Include toxicity detection and topic filtering.

exercises.title: Red-Team an LLM System
exercises.description: Systematically test an LLM application with adversarial prompts. Document vulnerabilities found and implement mitigations.

exercises.title: Build a Guardrails System
exercises.description: Create a guardrails framework that validates LLM outputs against safety criteria, enforces output formats, and provides configurable safety policies.

exercises.title: Run a Local LLM
exercises.description: Set up and run a quantized LLM locally using llama.cpp or Ollama. Test it with various prompts and measure performance metrics.

exercises.title: Build a Multi-Modal Application
exercises.description: Create an application that processes both text and images. Build an image description system that answers questions about uploaded images.

exercises.title: Implement Model Distillation
exercises.description: Distill knowledge from a larger model into a smaller one for a specific task. Compare performance, latency, and cost before and after distillation.

exercises.title: Project Proposal
exercises.description: Write a project proposal including: problem statement, target users, success criteria, high-level architecture, tech stack, and timeline.

exercises.title: Architecture Review
exercises.description: Present your architecture design for peer review. Get feedback on scalability, security, and technical decisions. Revise based on feedback.

exercises.title: Final Presentation
exercises.description: Prepare and deliver a 15-minute presentation of your capstone project. Include live demo, technical deep-dive, and lessons learned.

challenges.title: Build a Text Summarizer
challenges.description: Build a Python script that takes a long text as input and returns a summary using an LLM API. Support different summary lengths (short, medium, long).
requirements: Accept text input from file or stdin

challenges.title: Build a Mini Transformer
challenges.description: Implement a minimal transformer model from scratch using PyTorch. Train it on a simple text classification task and compare its performance to an RNN baseline.
requirements: Compare accuracy with a simple RNN

challenges.title: Build a Token Analyzer
challenges.description: Build a tool that analyzes tokenization efficiency for different providers. Input text and compare token counts, estimated costs, and context window utilization across multiple providers.
requirements: Show which tokens are most/least efficient
Visualize token boundaries in the original text

challenges.title: Build a Prompt Engineering Toolkit
challenges.description: Build a Python library for prompt engineering that includes: prompt templates, few-shot example management, chain-of-thought scaffolding, output parsing, and prompt versioning.
requirements: Prompt versioning and A/B testing support

challenges.title: Build a Fine-tuning Pipeline
challenges.description: Build an end-to-end fine-tuning pipeline that handles data preparation, training, evaluation, and deployment of a fine-tuned model.

challenges.title: Build an Enterprise RAG System
challenges.description: Build a production-quality RAG system with document ingestion, multiple chunking strategies, hybrid search, re-ranking, and citation support.
requirements: Support multiple document formats (PDF, MD, HTML)

challenges.title: Build an Autonomous Research Agent
challenges.description: Build an agent that takes a research question, searches the web, analyzes sources, and produces a well-structured report with citations.
requirements: Web search and content extraction tools

challenges.title: Build an Evaluation Framework
challenges.description: Build a comprehensive evaluation framework that supports automated metrics, human evaluation, and LLM-as-a-judge for any LLM application.

challenges.title: Build a Production LLM Service
challenges.description: Build a production-ready LLM service with API, caching, rate limiting, monitoring, fallbacks, and auto-scaling. Deploy it on a cloud platform.

challenges.title: Build a Safe LLM Application
challenges.description: Take a previously built LLM application and add comprehensive safety measures: content filtering, guardrails, adversarial testing, and monitoring.
requirements: Adversarial test suite with 50+ attack prompts

challenges.title: Build a Hybrid LLM System
challenges.description: Build a system that intelligently routes between local and cloud LLMs based on task complexity, privacy requirements, and latency constraints.

challenges.title: Capstone: Full-Stack LLM Application
challenges.description: Build, deploy, and present a complete LLM application that demonstrates mastery of all course topics.
requirements: Clear problem statement and target audience

interviews.question: Explain what an LLM is and how it differs from traditional ML models.
interviews.expectedAnswer: LLMs are large neural networks trained on massive text data to understand and generate language. Unlike traditional ML models that are task-specific, LLMs are general-purpose and can be adapted to many tasks.

interviews.question: What is the training process for an LLM?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Pre-training on massive text corpora to learn language patterns, then fine-tuning on task-specific data, and optionally RLHF to align with human preferences.

interviews.question: What are the main challenges in deploying LLMs in production?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Cost, latency, memory, safety, hallucination, keeping models updated, and handling edge cases.

interviews.question: Explain the self-attention mechanism in detail.
interviews.expectedAnswer: Self-attention computes Q, K, V matrices from input embeddings. Attention scores are QK^T/sqrt(d_k), softmaxed, then used to weight V. This lets each token attend to all others based on relevance.

interviews.question: Why is scaling by sqrt(d_k) necessary in attention?
What are the advantages of decoder-only transformers for LLMs?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Simpler architecture, better scaling properties, natural autoregressive generation, unified training objective (next-token prediction), and ability to handle both understanding and generation tasks.

interviews.question: Why is tokenization important for LLM engineering?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Tokenization determines how text is processed, affects context window utilization, impacts API costs, and influences model performance especially for non-English text and code.

interviews.question: Explain how BPE tokenization works step by step.
interviews.expectedAnswer: Start with character-level tokens. Find the most frequent pair of adjacent tokens. Merge them into a new token. Repeat until desired vocabulary size is reached.

interviews.question: How would you optimize tokenization for a multilingual application?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Choose a tokenizer trained on diverse multilingual data, consider vocabulary size trade-offs, evaluate tokenization rates per language, and possibly use language-specific tokenizers.

interviews.question: How would you improve a prompt that produces inconsistent outputs?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Add few-shot examples, be more specific about output format, use structured output requests, reduce temperature, and add explicit constraints.

interviews.question: When would you use chain-of-thought vs few-shot prompting?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Use CoT for complex reasoning tasks. Use few-shot for classification, formatting, and pattern matching. They can be combined for maximum effectiveness.

interviews.question: How do you handle prompt injection attacks?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Validate inputs, use system prompts to set boundaries, implement output filtering, separate user input from instructions, and test adversarial inputs.

interviews.question: When would you choose fine-tuning over prompt engineering?
interviews.expectedAnswer: When prompting can't achieve the desired accuracy, when you need consistent output formats, when latency is critical, or when you need domain-specific knowledge embedded in the model.

interviews.question: Explain LoRA and its advantages.
interviews.expectedAnswer: LoRA freezes the base model and trains low-rank adapter matrices. Advantages: fewer trainable parameters, less memory, faster training, and ability to swap adapters for different tasks.

interviews.question: How do you evaluate if fine-tuning was successful?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Compare metrics (accuracy, loss, task-specific metrics) before and after fine-tuning on a held-out test set. Check for overfitting by monitoring train vs validation loss.

interviews.question: Explain the RAG pipeline from document ingestion to answer generation.
interviews.expectedAnswer: Documents are chunked, embedded, and stored in a vector database. Queries are embedded and matched against stored vectors. Top chunks are retrieved, optionally re-ranked, and passed as context to the LLM.

interviews.question: When would you choose RAG over fine-tuning?
interviews.expectedAnswer: RAG when knowledge changes frequently, needs citations, or requires high accuracy on specific documents. Fine-tuning when behavior changes are needed or when latency is critical.

interviews.question: How do you handle multi-turn conversations in RAG?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Use conversation history to reformulate queries, combine current query with previous context for better retrieval, and maintain conversation state across RAG calls.

interviews.question: Explain the ReAct pattern and when to use it.
interviews.expectedAnswer: ReAct interleaves reasoning and action. The LLM thinks about what to do, takes an action, observes the result, and repeats. Best for tasks requiring external tool interaction and adaptive planning.

interviews.question: How do you ensure agent safety?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Implement permission systems, sandbox tool execution, set action limits, log all actions, require human approval for high-risk actions, and test adversarial scenarios.

interviews.question: When would you use multi-agent vs single-agent systems?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Multi-agent for complex tasks requiring diverse expertise, parallel execution, or separation of concerns. Single-agent for simpler tasks where overhead isn't justified.

interviews.question: How do you evaluate an LLM application that has no ground truth labels?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Use LLM-as-a-judge, human evaluation, proxy metrics (consistency, relevance scores), A/B testing with users, and task-specific heuristic metrics.

interviews.question: What metrics matter most for a chatbot?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Response quality, relevance, safety, consistency, latency, user satisfaction, task completion rate, and conversation coherence.

interviews.question: How do you handle evaluation at scale?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Automate with LLM-as-a-judge, sample from production traffic, use statistical significance testing, and build evaluation pipelines that run on every deployment.

interviews.question: How would you design a production LLM service?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Layered architecture with API gateway, business logic, LLM abstraction layer, caching, monitoring. Include error handling, fallbacks, rate limiting, and observability.

interviews.question: How do you reduce LLM API costs?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Implement caching, optimize prompts for token efficiency, use model routing, batch requests, stream responses, and monitor cost per request.

interviews.question: What metrics should you monitor in production?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Latency, throughput, error rates, cost per request, token usage, cache hit rates, quality scores, and user satisfaction metrics.

interviews.question: How would you protect an LLM application from prompt injection?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Separate user input from instructions, validate/sanitize inputs, use output filtering, implement guardrails, test adversarially, and limit model permissions.

interviews.question: What is the role of red-teaming in LLM safety?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Red-teaming proactively finds vulnerabilities through adversarial testing. It helps identify safety issues before deployment, guiding mitigation strategies.

interviews.question: How do you balance safety with functionality?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Implement safety measures proportionate to risk. Use layered defenses, allow users to adjust safety levels appropriately, and monitor for false positives.

interviews.question: When would you choose local deployment over cloud APIs?
interviews.expectedAnswer: For privacy-sensitive data, low-latency requirements, high-volume processing, offline capability, or when API costs exceed hardware investment.

interviews.question: Explain quantization and its impact on model quality.
interviews.expectedAnswer: Quantization reduces weight precision. 4-bit quantization reduces model size by 8x with minimal quality loss. Lower precision may affect complex reasoning tasks.

interviews.question: How do multi-modal models work architecturally?
interviews.expectedAnswer: They use encoders for each modality, align representations in a shared space, and use the LLM for reasoning across modalities. Vision encoders process images into tokens the LLM can understand.

interviews.question: Walk me through your capstone project architecture.
interviews.expectedAnswer: Explain the problem, your solution approach, key components, data flow, technology choices, and trade-offs. Focus on why you made each decision.

interviews.question: What was the biggest technical challenge in your capstone?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Describe the challenge, your approach to solving it, alternatives considered, and what you learned. Show problem-solving process.

interviews.question: What would you do differently if you started over?
interviews.expectedAnswer: Reflect on mistakes, missed opportunities, and lessons learned. Show growth mindset and self-awareness about technical decisions.

glossary.definition: Large Language Model — a neural network trained on massive text data to understand and generate human language.
The architecture underlying modern LLMs, based on self-attention mechanisms.
A chunk of text that the model processes. Can be a word, part of a word, or a character.
The maximum number of tokens an LLM can process in a single request.
The input text provided to an LLM to generate a response.
Adapting a pre-trained model to specific tasks using task-specific data.
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — aligning model outputs with human preferences.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation — combining LLMs with external knowledge retrieval.
A numerical representation of text in a vector space.
The process of generating outputs from a trained model.
Low-Rank Adaptation — a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that trains small adapter matrices.
Reducing model weight precision (e.g., to 4-bit) to decrease size and memory requirements.
A mechanism allowing each token to compute relevance scores with every other token in a sequence.
Byte Pair Encoding — a subword tokenization algorithm that iteratively merges frequent byte pairs.
When an LLM generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect information.
Constraints and checks that ensure LLM outputs meet safety, quality, and compliance requirements.
An autonomous system using an LLM as a reasoning engine to plan, decide, and execute tasks with tools.
A prompting technique that asks the model to show reasoning step by step before giving an answer.
Training a smaller model to mimic a larger teacher model's behavior, transferring knowledge.
Sending LLM response tokens to the user as they're generated rather than waiting for completion.
